title: Aliasing Tables
description: A clever shortcut to save you from typing too much.

slides.title: So much typing 💻
slides.content:
# My fingers hurt!

When you join big tables, Dot Notation becomes incredibly exhausting.

`SELECT employees.first_name, departments.name FROM employees INNER JOIN departments ON employees.department_id = departments.id;`

That is so overwhelmingly annoying to type out!

slides.title: Table Aliases 🕵️‍♂️
slides.content:
## Nicknaming Tables

Just like how we gave columns nicknames with `AS`, you can casually nickname your tables too! You don't even need the word `AS`. Just put a letter after the table name in the `FROM/JOIN` clauses.

`FROM employees e INNER JOIN departments d`

Now, `e` stands for employees, and `d` stands for departments!

quizData.question: How does aliasing drastically improve code readability?

options.text: It instantly deletes the secondary table
It turns long table names (`departments`) into tiny 1-letter variables (`d`), making Dot Notation much faster to read and write

quizData.explanation: Writing `SELECT e.first_name, d.name ... ON e.dept_id = d.id` is vastly cleaner and more efficient.

slides.title: The Self Join 🪞
slides.content:
## A weird edge case

Sometimes, a table needs to join *to itself*. Imagine an `employees` table where every row has a `manager_id`. But the manager is ALSO an employee in the same table!

To join it, you MUST use aliases to pretend it's two different tables: 
`SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager FROM employees e LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;`

quizData.question: If you need to join an `employees` table strictly to itself to find managers, why MUST you employ aliases?

options.text: Because it speeds up the math calculation
Because otherwise the SQL engine wouldn't know which 'employees' table you were talking about when matching the IDs

quizData.explanation: By aliasing the singular table as 'e' and 'm', the database briefly pretends it has two distinct tables and can safely compare them.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.codeSnippet:
SELECT p.name, t.city
FROM players p
INNER JOIN teams t
  ON p.team_id = t.id;
